fact: March 26 sits exactly 5 days after the spring equinox, which means day and night are getting seriously unbalanced - daylight is winning the battle!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime time for earthworms to go absolutely bonkers, wiggling up from deep soil as it finally warms up enough for their slimy adventures.
Cherry blossom trees in Japan are either exploding with pink flowers right now or getting ready to - March 26 falls smack in the middle of sakura season!
Why do cats seem extra crazy around this time? March 26 falls during peak 'spring fever' season when animals get hyperactive from changing daylight hours messing with their brains.
The sun rises about 2 minutes earlier and sets about 2 minutes later than it did just yesterday - that's 4 whole minutes of extra daylight gained in a single day!
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere places, March 26 means autumn is kicking in hard - leaves are turning colors and falling like confetti.
This date lands right in the middle of Aries season, making it a peak time for the zodiac's most impatient and energetic sign - no wonder everyone feels restless!
Maple trees are pumping out sweet sap like crazy right now in cold regions - March 26 is prime maple syrup making time when trees are basically bleeding sugar water.
The moon phase around March 26 changes every year, but when it's a new moon near this date, the night sky becomes a perfect black canvas for seeing the most stars.
Hibernating animals like bears and groundhogs are either already awake or stirring in their dens - March 26 falls during the 'great awakening' period when sleepy creatures rejoin the world.
In northern places, this is when ice on lakes and ponds starts making the weirdest creaking and cracking sounds as it begins to melt and shift.
March 26 marks day 85 of the year in regular years and day 86 in leap years - either way, you've officially survived more than 23% of the entire year!
